coredata.dc:title: Cyan-emitting silicon quantum dots as a fluorescent probe directly used for highly sensitive and selective detection of chlorogenic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yulu
Cao, Lei
Zan, Minghui
Peng, Jiahui
Wang, Panyong
Pang, Xinpei
Zhang, Yan
Li, Li
Dong, Wen-Fei
Mei, Qian
coredata.dc:description: As an important bioactive component in plants, chlorogenic acid (CGA) has been widely studied for its potential role in human health. In this work, cyan fluorescent silicon quantum dots were successfully synthesized via a simple one-pot method for the rapid detection of CGA. The optimal excitation and emission wavelength of the obtained SiQDs was 350 nm and 470 nm, respectively. When the CGA was added, the maximum emission intensity of the SiQDs can be effectively quenched due to dynamic and static mixed quenching mechanisms. More significantly, there was a remarkable linear correlation between fluorescence quenching efficiency and a broad concentration of CGA solution range from 10 to 150 μmol/L with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.43 μmol/L. Furthermore, the proposed SiQDs were successfully applied to analyze CGA in coffee beans and instant coffee after simple pretreatment with satisfactory results. Based on these, a high sensitivity and excellent selectivity fluorescent probe detection system was constructed, and it provides a valuable platform for the detection of CGA and has broad application prospects in the biological and pharmaceutical analysis field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicon quantum dots
Chlorogenic acid
Fluorescence quenching
Static quenching
Dynamic quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of CGA in aqueous solution
Determination of CGA in the real samples
Fluorescence determination for CGA with SiQDs
Selectivity of the proposed method for CGA
Application of CGA in actual samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The Lewis structure of chlorogenic acid.
(A) TEM images of SiQDs (Inset: the HRTEM image); (B) Particle size distribution histogram of SiQDs; (C) FTIR spectrum of SiQDs. (D) XPS survey spectra of SiQDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra (black line), fluorescent excitation (red line) and emission (blue line) spectra of SiQDs. (Inset: Images of SiQDs solution under daylight (left) and UV light (λ = 365 nm, right) respectively); (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of SiQDs under different excitation wavelengths (330–410 nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence excitation spectra of SiQDs with different concentrations of CGA (from top to bottom, 0 and 150 μmol/L). Inset: Images of the SiQDs in the absence (left) and presence (right) of CGA under UV light (λ = 365 nm). (B) Logarithmic value of F
0
/F of SiQDs as a function of CGA concentration of 0–150 μmol/L. F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of SiQDs in the absence and presence of CGA, respectively.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of SiQDs (black line), CGA (red line) and SiQDs ​+ ​CGA (blue line). (B) Fluorescence lifetime curve of SiQDs and SiQDs ​+ ​CGA.
Schematic illustration about synthetic strategy of the SiQDs and detection principle for CGA.

head.ce:title: Cyan-emitting silicon quantum dots as a fluorescent probe directly used for highly sensitive and selective detection of chlorogenic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biomedical Engineering (Suzhou), Division of Life Sciences and Medicine, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, 230026, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Division of Life Sciences and Medicine
University of Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biomedical Engineering (Suzhou), Division of Life Sciences and Medicine, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, 230026, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics, Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology, Chinese Academy of Science (CAS), Suzhou, 215163, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics
Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics, Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology, Chinese Academy of Science (CAS), Suzhou, 215163, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As an important bioactive component in plants, chlorogenic acid (CGA) has been widely studied for its potential role in human health. In this work, cyan fluorescent silicon quantum dots were successfully synthesized via a simple one-pot method for the rapid detection of CGA. The optimal excitation and emission wavelength of the obtained SiQDs was 350 nm and 470 nm, respectively. When the CGA was added, the maximum emission intensity of the SiQDs can be effectively quenched due to dynamic and static mixed quenching mechanisms. More significantly, there was a remarkable linear correlation between fluorescence quenching efficiency and a broad concentration of CGA solution range from 10 to 150 μmol/L with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.43 μmol/L. Furthermore, the proposed SiQDs were successfully applied to analyze CGA in coffee beans and instant coffee after simple pretreatment with satisfactory results. Based on these, a high sensitivity and excellent selectivity fluorescent probe detection system was constructed, and it provides a valuable platform for the detection of CGA and has broad application prospects in the biological and pharmaceutical analysis field.

ce:section.ce:para: As the second-largest element in the earth's crust, silicon has been widely used in all aspects of life. Besides, silicon-based nanomaterials have attracted much interest due to their excellent optical, electronic and mechanical properties [1]. Silicon quantum dots (SiQDs), a new type of fluorescent probe with high fluorescence quantum yield, have attracted extensive attention in many fields because of their water solubility, unique optical properties, high light stability and good surface tolerability [2].In addition, the distinct advantage of SiQDs is nontoxic because it could be metabolized in vivo and the biodegraded product, orthosilicic acid, which is readily excreted in the urine [3]. Based on these characteristics, silicon quantum dots have been widely used in the fluorescence detection of a variety of materials such as alkaline phosphatase [4], glutathione [5], NO2 − [6], chromium [7], dopamine [8] and cysteine [9]. However, the application of SiQDs in the detection of drug components is relatively rare.

Chlorogenic acid (CGA), an ester formed between caffeic acid and quinic acid (Fig. 1 ), which is a kind of phenylpropanoid compound widely existing in various plants [10]. In addition, CGA has a high content in many medicinal plants such as Honeysuckle, eucommia and other traditional Chinese medicines [11]. As the main bioactive component, CGA has a wide range of functions, such as antibacterial [12], antiviral [13], blood pressure lowering blood lipids [14], antioxidant [15], scavenging free radicals [16], etc. On account of this, the research on the bioactivity of CGA has been carried out from many aspects, among which the analytical methods of CGA content have attracted much attention. The traditional detection methods include high performance liquid chromatography [17], micellar electrokinetic chromatography [18], thin-layer scanning [19], liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry [20] and electrochemical method [21]. However, most of these methods were expensive, complex in operation, and low efficiency, which are not conducive to the practical application of conventional analysis. In recent years, the emergence of fluorescent sensors provides a new field for CGA detection. For example, Yang et al. synthesized fluorescent carbon dots based on the principle of IFE to analyze the content of CGA [22]. Liu et al. synthesized nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots, and established CGA probe sensor based on the ratiometric fluorescence method [23]. However, there are seldom reports on using silicon quantum dots as probes for CGA analysis.

In this work, cyan-emitting silicon quantum dots to analyze the content of CGA were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using 3-Aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES) and o-phenylenediamine as precursors, with PEI as passivator agent. The obtained SiQDs exhibits excellent stability in sodium chloride solution and different pH and has rapid response speed with simple operation. Besides, the fluorescence was selectively quenched by CGA through the synergistic way of DQE (dynamic quenching) and SQE (static quenching). The results showed the strong linear relationship in a series of gradient concentration CGA solutions with the fluorescence quenching efficiency of SiQDs, and the detection limit was calculated to be 0.43 μmol/L. The low LOD value indicated that the SiQDs were sensitive enough for the quantification of CGA analysis in real samples. Finally, the coffee beans and instant coffee were tested as the real sample with recoveries of 99.96–109.20%, which provide a novel and reliable method for the application of CGA detection.

Chlorogenic acid (CGA) was obtained from Maclean Biochemical Technology (Shanghai, China). 3-Aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES), o-Phenylenediamine and Polyethyleneimine (PEI) were purchased from Titan Scientific (Shanghai, China). HCl, NaOH, NaCl, NaF, NaBr, NaI, KCl, CaCl2, Pb(NO3)2, Ba(NO3)2, C4H6O4Zn, and CdH8N2O10 were purchased from Energy Chemical (Shanghai, China), AlCl3, MnCl2, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, NaNO2, NaNO3, C2H3NaO2, Na2SO3, Na2SO4, Na3PO4, NaClO, Glutathione (GSH), Glucose, Sucrose, Urea, Cys and Gly were all acquired from Aladdin Chemistry (Shanghai, China). All chemicals used in this experiment were of analytical grade and used directly without further purification, and ultrapure water was used throughout.

The morphologies and the size of the SiQDs were recorded using TECNAI G20 high resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEI, USA) at an acceleration voltage of 100 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured on a VERTEX 70 FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra was conducted using ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). Fluorescence lifetime was acquired by FLS 1000 (Edinburgh, UK) at room temperature. The UV absorption spectra were analyzed via a Hitachi UV2450 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). All fluorescence spectra were performed by F97Pro FL spectrophotometer (Lengguang Technology, Shanghai) equipped with a 1.0 cm quartz cell.

The SiQDs were synthesized by one simple one-pot hydrothermal method. Firstly, 1 mL APTES was added to 10 mL ultrapure water and 0.2 g of o-Phenylenediamine was dissolved in 10.0 mL anhydrous ethanol. Next, the two solutions were mixed under the ultrasonic condition and 0.2 g of PEI was added through stirring for 2 min. Then the resulting mixture was treated hydrothermally in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 200 °C for 8 h. After the reaction, the SiQDs were cooled to room temperature and purified against water using a dialysis bag (1000 Da, molecular weight cutoff) for about 6 h to remove residual reagents. Finally, the purified solution was stored at 4 °C for further use or freeze dried in a vacuum freeze dryer to collect solid powder for further characterization.

The fluorescence quantum yield of the SiQDs were measured by comparative method using quinine sulfate as a reference standard (QY: 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4) [24]. To avoid reabsorption, the absorbance value of the standard solution and the SiQDs were required to be smaller than 0.10. The quantum yield value was based on the following equation: Qx =
Qr
IxAr IrAx
.where Q is the quantum yield, A is absorbance and I referred to the peak area of fluorescence spectrum. The subscripts r and x indicate the standard and the tested samples, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CGA in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: Samples with 1 mL SiQDs stock solution was diluted to 3 mL with ultrapure water, then a series of different concentrations of CGA solution were added to a quartz cuvette containing 3 mL of the above SiQDs solutions sequentially. After the mixed solution was shaken thoroughly, the fluorescence emission spectra of each sample were recorded under the fluorescence excitation wavelength at 350 nm. In order to detect the sensitivity of CDs to CGA, some inorganic ion solutions and organic molecules as interfering substances were chosen under the same conditions. All experiments were conducted at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of CGA in the real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to confirm the practicability of the proposed fluorescent probe, the coffee beans and instant coffee were selected for the analysis of chlorogenic acid content. Green coffee beans were obtained online (Yunnan, China), the instant coffee was purchased from the local supermarket, and the roasted beans were come by baking green beans at 200 °C for 15 minutes. Both green and roasted coffee beans were pulverized to a powder and dried in a vacuum oven at 85 °C for 6h. The green coffee beans solution was prepared by weighing 0.1 g of coffee powder and dissolving in 10 mL ultrapure water, and then it was treated by ultrasonic treatment for 30 minutes after stirring. After that, the solution above was first filtered through filter paper and then filtered through a 0.22 μm polystyrene filter to acquire a transparent solution without impurity and insoluble matter, roasted beans and instant coffee were treated in the same way, and then the obtained samples were used for fluorescence analysis. Considering the different content of chlorogenic acid in different coffee samples, a diverse amount of coffee sample solution was taken for determination. 50μL of green beans and roasted beans samples, 300 μL of instant coffee samples were mixed with 3.0 mL SiQDs diluted solution in each group. In order to ensure the results more reliable, the fluorescence intensity of each group was determined by five times.

As depicted in Scheme 1 , the SiQDs were synthesized with a straightforward one-pot hydrothermal method using APTES and o-phenylenediamine as precursors, and the PEI was chosen as the passivator to improve their fluorescence intensity and enhance their water solubility. The morphology and size distribution of the SiQDs were observed by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 2 A, the SiQDs shows a satisfactory uniformity, and their size range from 28.6 nm to 33.8 nm with an average diameter of about 30.5 nm, which is smaller than the results (52.8 nm) determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS, Fig. 2B), this is because of the different surface states under the two tested conditions [25]. Meanwhile, it can be seen that some of the SiQDs are crystalline and the lattice plane spacing was measured to be 0.31 nm. The Fourier Transform Infrared spectrum (FTIR) measurements were also used to identify the surface groups and the bonding composition of SiQDs. As illustrated in Fig. 2C, the absorption peak at 3334 cm−1 and 3283 cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H, respectively [26]. The absorption peaks at 2928 cm−1 and 2814 cm−1 were attributed to C–H unsaturated stretching vibration and the peak at 1458 cm−1 corresponded to C–N vibration [27]. The signal at 1632 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of CO [28]. Most importantly, the strong absorbance at 1092 cm−1 and 1007 cm−1 ascribed to the Si–O bending vibrations that proved successfully prepare SiQDs [29]. These results demonstrated that there were plenty of hydroxyl groups and amino groups on the surface of the SiQDs, and these functional hydrophilic groups on the surface greatly implied the excellent water solubility of the SiQDs.

For exploring the elemental composition and chemical bonds, the XPS spectra of SiQDs were recorded and presented in Fig. 2D. Five peaks at 102.5, 153.6, 285.6, 399.6 and 531.6 eV, were attributed to Si2p, Si2s, C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. Besides, the corresponding contents were 53.48% (C), 34.44% (N), 10.24% (O) and 1.84% (Si), respectively. In detail, the high resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. S1A) exhibited four fitted peaks which were ascribed to C–Si (284.5 eV), C–N (285.9 eV), C–O (286.7 eV) and CO (287.8 eV) [30]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. S1B) exhibited three peaks at 398.8 eV, 399.5 eV and 400.4 eV, which could be assigned to N–Si, C–N–C, and N-(C3) groups, respectively [9]. In addition, three peaks appeared in O 1s (Fig. S1C) spectrum, which 530.8 eV and 531.7 eV are assigned to Si–O while the peak 532.7 eV is signified to C–O [29]. Simultaneously, two peaks at 530.7 and 531.7 eV indicated the presence of the Si–O group (Fig. S1D), and the peak at 532.7 eV corresponded to Si–N [31]. The results of the XPS above were in agreement with the FT-IR analysis.

The optical properties of the obtained SiQDs were evaluated by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. A typical absorption band centered at about 290 nm was presented in the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 3 A), which can be ascribed to the π - π∗ transitions of CC bonds [32]. Fig. 3B was the fluorescence emission spectrum of the SiQDs at various excitation wavelengths (390–470 nm). The maximum emission peak which was at 470 nm can be obtained under excitation at 350 nm, consequently, 350 nm was chosen as the optimal excitation wavelength for the following experiments. Meanwhile, the aqueous solution of SiQDs was light yellow and transparent under natural light but emitted cyan fluorescence under the excitation of 350 nm wavelength (inset of Fig. 3A). The emission quantum yield (QY) of SiQDs was determined by using quinine sulfate (Φ R = 0.54) solution dissolved in 0.1 M sulfuric acid as a reference, and the QY of SiQDs was calculated to be 10.73%.

In order to ensure that the detection effect of the quantum dots is more accurate and reliable, a variety of experimental methods were utilized to verify the stability of SiQDs. As shown in Fig. S2A, the fluorescence intensity was nearly constant in the range of pH 3 to 12 and only changed in the extreme environment of pH 2 and 13, which indicated that the SiQDs have an ideal pH stability in a wide range. Meanwhile, Fig. S2B shows the photostability of SiQDs, it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity remains at a high level after 2 hours of UV irradiation (λ = 365 nm). Finally, the sodium chloride tolerance test was carried out on the SiQDs (Fig. S2C), even if the concentration of NaCl medium was as high as 2 mol/L in the solution, the fluorescence emission of SiQDs has no significant change, which confirmed the excellent sodium chloride resistance made the as-prepared SiQDs. All the above results fully suggested the excellent fluorescence stability of the synthesized SiQDs for their applications in real environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence determination for CGA with SiQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to make the detection more reliable, the effects of reaction time and pH on CGA detection were further investigated. As shown in Fig. S3A, the fluorescence intensity decreased sharply after adding CGA after 1 min and keeps stable in the following hour, which certificated excellent and quick response ability of the SiQDs and significantly provided the possibility for rapid detection. In addition, it can be seen that the reaction system is almost unaffected by pH and has good stability in a wide range of pH (Fig. S3B). The above results further confirmed the sensitivity and stability of the SiQDs detection system.

Then the relationship between the fluorescence intensity of SiQDs and the concentration of CGA was tested. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity of SiQDs decreased gradually with the increasing amounts of CGA. At the same time, the illustration showed that the cyan fluorescence finally changed to colorless with the addition of CGA, indicating that CGA can effectively inhibit the fluorescence of SiQDs. The quenching efficiency Lg (F
0
/F) has an excellent linear relationship with CGA concentration in the range of 10–150 μmol/L (Fig. 4B) with the limit of detection at 0.43 μmol/L. The linear regression equation was Lg (F
0
/F) ​= ​0.00759c ​+ ​0.05382 (R2 = 0.997). Which F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of SiQDs in the absence and presence of CGA, respectively. Compared with previous studies (Table 1), the SiQDs fluorescence sensing of this method has a lower detection limit and wider linear range than that of most previous works.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the proposed method for CGA
ce:section.ce:para: The interference of impurities and potential substances is an important index affecting the detection results of the fluorescence sensor. Herein, the method of coexistence of multiple substances and detection system was used to detect the selectivity of SiQDs to CGA. The interfering substances used are various common material such as metal cations (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Mn2+), anions (CO3 2−, HCO3 −, NO2−, NO3 −, CH3COO−, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, PO4 2−, ClO−), and small molecule organic compounds(GSH, Glucose, Sucrose, Urea, Cys, Gly). The concentration of CGA was 50 μmol/L, and the concentration of interfering substance was 10 times than that of CGA. Results as shown in Fig. 5 , the addition of these interfering substances did not cause obvious fluorescence quenching, indicating that the SiQDs fluorescence sensor has high selectivity for CGA in complex environments.

In order to explore the quenching mechanisms of CGA and SiQDs, the mechanisms of each common quenching mode were used to verify the experimental system. Dynamic quenching and static quenching have widely existed in various fluorescence quantum dots. The two models can be verified by the Stern-Volmer (S–V) equation, namely F
0
/F ​= ​1 ​+ ​K[Q], where F
0 and F are the steady-state fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of the quencher respectively; K is the Stern-Volmer constant and [Q] is the concentration of the quencher [33]. Fig. S4A shows the relationship between F
0
/F and the concentration of CGA, it is obvious that the obtained curve was not a traditional linear relationship, but a curve bending to the y-axis, indicating that the quenching system can not be explained by a single quenching mode [34]. In addition, the upward curves of the S–V plot were an obvious sign of mixed quenching modes, it is preliminarily speculated that both dynamic quenching and static quenching participated in the quenching process [35]. The static quenching can be proved by observing the changes in the absorption spectra of the fluorophore. Fig. 6 A showed the UV absorption spectra of SiQDs, CGA and CGA ​+ ​SiQDs, respectively. It can be seen that a new absorption peak appeared after the addition of CGA (blue line), indicating the ground state complex was formed, while the absorption peak of SiQDs was weakened, which is consistent with the characteristics of static quenching [36]. In addition, in the dynamic quenching mode, the fluorophore loss the energy of the excited state due to the non-radiative route, which eventually lead to the change of fluorescence lifetime in the excited state. Therefore, detecting the changes of fluorescence lifetime is an effective method to demonstrate the dynamic quenching [37]. As shown in Fig. 6B, the obtained data were well fitted with dual-exponential decay analysis. The black line represented the fluorescence lifetime of SiQDs, and the two fluorescence decay components of CDs were 2.05 ns (20.50%) and 4.88 ns (79.50%), which the average fluorescence lifetime of SiQDs was about 4.30 ns. Besides, the red line represented the fluorescence decay curve of SiQDs ​+ ​CGA, and the two fluorescence decay components of this system were 0.60 ns (8.42%) and 3.95 ns (91.58%), which the average fluorescence lifetime was calculated to be 3.67 ns, it can be found that the fluorescence lifetime decreased with the addition of CGA, which confirmed the existence of dynamic quenching. These results further certificated that the fluorescence quenching of SiQDs by CGA was attributed to the dynamic and static mixed quenching mechanisms. Then, other possible quenching methods were also discussed. As shown in Fig. S4B, the characteristic absorption peak of CGA was between 275 and 375 nm, and there is a portion of overlap with the excitation spectrum of SiQDs at 350 nm, which conformed to the characteristics of internal filtering effect, indicating that IFE may exist at the same time [38]. The Förster resonance energy transfer requires the absorption spectrum of the quencher in the ground state effectively overlaps with the emission spectrum of quantum dots [39], however, the absorption peak of CGA was not meet the condition above, so the influence of FRET was eliminated. Therefore, the quenching principle of the system was a mixed quenching mode dominated by dynamic and static quenching processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CGA in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Chlorogenic acid is widely distributed in plants, and closely related to human life. In this experiment, coffee beans and instant coffee were selected as the actual sample for CGA testing, and a series of known concentrations of CGA was added in the sample for the recoveries of this method. The content of CGA in coffee was calculated by the above linear regression equation. As summarized in Table 2, the recovery rate of CGA in coffee was 99.96–109.20%, with the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.08%–1.36%. At the same time, it can be seen that the content of CGA in roasted beans and instant coffee were lower than that in green beans because of the loss of CGA caused by oxidation during roasting, which was consistent with the previous reports [40]. These results show that the fluorescence probe of these SiQDs has high precision and reliable results, which demonstrated that the SiQDs have potential application in the detection of CGA in real samples.

In summary, a novel silicon doped fluorescent quantum dots were successfully synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method using 3-Aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES) as the silicon source in this experiment. The obtained SiQDs exhibited excellent water solubility and good stability. Meanwhile, the Chlorogenic acid (CGA) detection system with SiQDs as the fluorescent probe showed the advantages of high selectivity, strong anti-interference ability, and quick response capability, the detection procedure can be completed and achieved stable equilibrium within 1 min. Significantly, CGA is a kind of dietary phenolic compound and coffee is a common source of CGA in the human diet, and the practical applications of SiQDs for the assay of CGA was successfully demonstrated in coffee with satisfactory recoveries. Based on the bioactivity and pharmaceutical value of CGA, this work has great practical value and potential application prospects in the quantitative detection of the CGA after extraction and purification. And this work also provides a new idea for the application of silicon quantum dots in the direction of actual drug components analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the National Key R&D Program of China (Grand No.2020YFC2004500), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grand No. 8160071152, 21803075, 61805273, and 81902166), the Primary Research & Development Plan of Jiangsu Province (BE2019683), the Academy-Locality Cooperation (2020SYHZ0041), and the Science and Technology Department of Jinan City (2018GXRC016).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2021.122465.

Panyong Wang: Software, Data curation. Xinpei Pang: Methodology, Data curation. Yan Zhang: Formal analysis. Li Li: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-pot synthesis of fluorescent silicon nanoparticles for sensitive and selective determination of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol in aqueous solution
Anal. Chem.
